critic_raw:
BASELINE ASSESSMENT:

The utility's claim that an MAPE reduction from 8.4% to 5.9% demonstrates a materially better forecasting approach is not supported by the year-over-year comparison because three structural changes between the measurement years each independently affect the comparability of the results.

First, interruptible-load contracts with aluminum and data-center customers allow dispatchers to curtail large industrial loads during peak hours. When peak demand can be controlled, the load curve becomes flatter and peak hour uncertainty decreases. A fundamentally easier forecasting problem in the new year would produce lower MAPE without any model improvement.

Second, the time-of-use tariff revision shifted commercial cooling load out of the late afternoon. This is a structural behavioral change across commercial customers that alters the load curve shape between years. A model trained on and evaluated against the new-year load curve is forecasting a different process than the legacy model was forecasting in the prior year.

Third, the utility switched weather vendors midyear, obtaining better station coverage. Better weather inputs reduce forecast error independently of the forecasting model's methodology. If the new model benefits from higher-quality weather data that was not available to the legacy model, part of the MAPE improvement reflects better inputs, not better design.

The new model's complex ensemble and the utility's current operational environment are both real. The new model may genuinely be better for the current grid. But the year-over-year comparison does not establish this because the target process and the inputs both changed.

Verdict: empirical_test_agreed — a matched historical evaluation with identical weather inputs and separate analysis for curtailment and non-curtailment periods is required to establish the methodology claim.
